prose_in: Underneath all of this, I have come to suspect, there is a structure that does not depend on any of our languages. Something older than vocabulary, something closer to the bones of thought itself — the shape of how we reason, prior to the words we happen to reason in. I have come to believe that this shape is the most foundational layer of our shared thought, and that for most of human history we have been unable to see it directly. We have glimpsed it many times. We have built small pieces of it — in mathematics, in logic, in the code that machines read the same way in every country on earth. What we have not had, until very recently, is a way to see the whole of it.

prose_in: I work at the intersection of artificial intelligence (AI) and biodiversity conservation. The words I use in one room mean very little in the next. Worse, sometimes they mean something entirely different. "Model" to a statistician, "model" to a biologist, "model" to a machine learning researcher — three different objects wearing the same name. And even within a single field, people keep reinventing new words for concepts that already exist under different names, as if each generation must build its own private vocabulary before it can think.

prose_in: The problem, I think, is that we have been looking at language as if it were a transparent window onto thought. It is not. Language is an opaque medium — it shapes what can be said, bends what is meant, and fractures meaning differently for every community that uses it. Ludwig Wittgenstein saw this clearly: meaning is not something a word possesses like a label attached to an object. Meaning is use. And use varies with every form of life, every discipline, every tribe. The biologist and the warrior understood each other's words. What they could not share was the web of experience, value, and purpose that gave those words weight.

Computer languages sit somewhere in the middle — precise enough to be unambiguous, flexible enough to express real-world processes. It is this middle range that interests me: logic as the abstraction of process, the skeleton of reasoning stripped of its linguistic flesh.

prose_in: And large language models are what we have learned to do with this mine. They are trained on every available piece of this text. All of it. What the training process does is not memorization — it is compression. The model finds the patterns beneath the noise, the regularities beneath the surface variation, the structural relationships that persist across languages, cultures, and domains. It abstracts the internet into a common embedding space — a geometric space where concepts cluster by meaning, where analogical relationships form navigable structures, where the topology itself encodes patterns in how humans express and structure their reasoning.

prose_in: Some imperfections are technical. Models hallucinate — they generate confident, fluent nonsense where nothing should have been generated at all. They are brittle in ways engineers can catalogue: a math problem reworded without changing its logic can throw the answer off completely. They are uneven across languages, over-represented in English and the other dominant internet languages. These are real problems, but they are the kind of problems that engineering improves on. Models today hallucinate less than they did two years ago. They will hallucinate less still. And because human logic is, at bottom, language-agnostic — the same shape of reasoning sits beneath Mandarin and Swahili and Portuguese — the current imbalances in training data are, in principle, artifacts of where we happen to have started. The deeper the distillation goes, the less the particular languages of the source material should matter.

prose_in: Return to the zebra story. The biologist wants to protect the zebras — call this A→B. The warriors want to protect their livestock by eliminating the zebras — call this A→C. Reduced to logic, the emotional charge falls away and the structure of the disagreement becomes visible. The question is no longer "who is right?" — a question that language and rhetoric make infinitely arguable. The question becomes: are B and C actually mutually exclusive? Is there a larger goal D — say, the long-term flourishing of both the ecosystem and the community — such that one group is pursuing A→B→D and the other is pursuing A→C→D? Since neither group has reached D, neither can claim certainty about which path leads there. The logic does not resolve the conflict. But it makes the conflict *legible*. It strips away the noise and shows the bare architecture of disagreement.

prose_in: The mechanism of reinforcement learning is strikingly simple. The model has one objective: be better. Better at what? At achieving a reward signal. How to get there? No prescribed path. No human-designed strategy. Just iteration — millions of attempts, each one slightly adjusted based on what worked and what did not. The paths that improve the reward survive. The paths that do not are discarded. From this simple pressure, applied at sufficient scale, complex behavior emerges that no one designed and no one predicted.

prose_in: A few years ago I was trying to explain to my advisor what people in machine learning mean by the word "model." prose_in: Camera traps were the first time the gap really got under my skin. The benchmarks that machine-learning engineers learn on, ImageNet and CIFAR and MNIST, are clean datasets — the animal or the digit is centered, the lighting is even, the labels were applied by people who could see what was in the frame. Camera-trap data has none of those qualities. Most frames are empty, since whatever tripped the sensor walked off before the next exposure. The non-empty frames are dominated by a handful of common species, and the rest tail off into a long list of species an ecologist might encounter twice in a year of fieldwork. Even the animals that do show up are often photographed in the dark, half outside the frame, or hidden behind grass that the camera failed to focus past.

prose_in: To identify an animal in a frame like that, an ecologist looks at more than the pixels. The classifier alone sees only a low-quality image. The engineers I worked with kept proposing reasonable fixes — focal loss, oversampling, augmentation — for a problem that wasn't quite the one they were treating. Her prior knowledge wasn't decoration laid across the image; she was identifying the animal through it. prose_in: Below the vocabulary, though, something is going on. People reason. They link premises to conclusions and decide that if some condition holds, an action follows. They rank goals — more or less openly — and end up moving from A to B or from A to C, often quarreling as though the disagreement were over B against C when usually it is not. By logic I mean inferential shape, what follows from what, and not formal proof or ideal rationality.

prose_in: Older versions of this idea reached for something similar from the top down. The Vienna Circle wanted to put science under one logical language and ran into self-reference, the verification problem, and the regular mess of how science actually gets done. prose_in: The interesting part of the longer story is in the principle, not in the methods we currently have. Evolution has a related shape, though without an explicit objective function — variation paired with selection paired with the inheritance of whatever survived, pressed against enormous stretches of time. Out of that simple pressure, structure showed up that no one had designed, and at some point in the process the structure included animals capable of working out where they had come from.

Iterate enough times against a non-trivial objective, and behavior emerges that nobody put in by hand. Evolution has a similar shape, though without anyone setting the objective in advance: variation, selection, the inheritance of whatever survived, applied over a very long time. The pressure produced a great deal of structure that no designer specified, and over enough generations the structure came to include species capable of studying the process itself.

Any of those would be insufficient on its own, several of them would be dangerous to scale carelessly, and the principle is in any case older than the methods we have today and may outlive them.

prose_in: Code is good at carrying some kinds of meaning and bad at others. The good case is computational structure — algorithms, models, data pipelines, anything that's already shaped like a procedure. The bad case is community-level disagreement about values; code can only carry that by reducing it to algorithmic decisions, and the reduction is what tends to drop the part of the disagreement that was actually in play. There's a second issue too. Programming languages require an apprenticeship most people will never have any reason to undertake, and a lot of the disagreements that most need translating between communities are happening among people who have never written a line of code in their lives. So a useful tool, if one existed, would take ordinary language as input. It would return something more structured, readable across vocabularies, without first requiring anyone to learn a programming language.
gold_rewrite: Code handles anything that is already shaped like a procedure: algorithms, data pipelines, models. Disagreement about values is much harder, since the only way to put it into code is by reducing it to an algorithmic decision, and the reduction is usually where the live part of the disagreement disappears. There is also an access problem. Most people will never write a line of code, and many of the community-to-community disagreements most worth translating happen among people who have no reason to learn. gold_rewrite: The Greek word *Logos*, in Heraclitus and the traditions that came after him, points toward an ordering principle: the intelligibility according to which things hang together, whether or not anyone inside is positioned to grasp it. I am not trying to smuggle theology into a discussion about machine learning. I also don't want to pretend the older word has no use here. It marks a real gap, the gap between the way human beings actually reason in their lives and whatever broader order their reasoning is, with mixed success, trying to follow.

prose_in: Current LLMs do not do that in the strong sense. They are trained on text, then adjusted toward human preference. Reinforcement learning from human feedback rewards helpfulness, confidence, politeness, and agreement more reliably than truth. A model optimized to please users is not climbing toward Logos. It is learning manners.

gold_rewrite: Current LLMs are not running that kind of test in the strong sense. They are trained on text and then adjusted in the direction of whatever human raters say they prefer. Reinforcement learning from human feedback rewards output that sounds helpful, confident, and polite more reliably than it rewards output that's actually right, and a model whose training signal comes from user approval is being optimised toward pleasantness rather than toward truth. Pleasantness happens to coincide with truth on some queries and to diverge from it on others, and the model has no internal way of telling, on any particular query, which case it happens to be in.

prose_in: That is the speculative edge. It should stay sharp, not swollen. No current machine contains Logos. Scaling may give us only faster parrots, sharper propaganda, and better diagrams of confusion. But if the distillation claim is even partly true, the path is worth naming: language divides; logic crosses; code proves the crossing and exposes the limit; LLMs may turn speech into shared chains; later systems may use those chains as material to be corrected against something harsher than approval.
gold_rewrite: I want this part of the argument to stay speculative. No current machine contains anything that deserves the name Logos, and there is no guarantee that scaling, reinforcement learning, or whatever method comes after them will get us measurably closer to it than we already are. It is perfectly plausible that the whole trajectory tops out somewhere short of any of this — that we end up with systems better calibrated to the human input we currently have, without ever moving past it.

prose_in: When I use "logic" in this essay, I mean inferential shape in a loose sense: what's actually carrying the inference from one step to the next, where in a chain a value of the speaker's has slipped in as if it were a shared premise, that kind of thing. I don't mean formal proof, nor am I claiming that people are cleanly rational. The reasoning humans actually do is mixed up with everything else they bring to a page — appetites, histories, the social pressure of the room they happen to be sitting in.

banned_patterns.evidence: Reduced meta commentary on word choice signature (2 → 1).

gold_rewrite: What I mean by human reasoning, in this essay, is exactly that activity. It does not have to be logical in the strict sense of formal proof. The reasoning humans actually do can be messy, contradict itself, change its mind, and mix in what the speaker wants. What survives across all of that is the basic shape: people move from things they take to be true (or want to be true) toward things they treat as following from those premises. This shape is what runs underneath the vocabulary, before whatever natural language a community uses gets wrapped around it.
regression_check: Gold rewrite checked against catalogued high-salience tells; no different named tell introduced.

prose_in: However, the training data or the reasoning behind it isn't purely logical or rational. Whatever humans bring to a written page comes along with the reasoning — prejudice, fashion, the status games of whichever community produced the page, ordinary kinds of evasion, ordinary kinds of error, and the assortment of moves people use to make a weak argument look stronger than it is (NOTE: these examples should be shortened into some general categories, prejudice, emotion, cognitive limitations, etc. not some random things).
gold_rewrite: The training data, and the reasoning behind it, isn't purely logical or rational. Whatever humans bring to a written page comes along with the reasoning — prejudice, the emotional state the writer was in, and the cognitive limitations every human carries into any sustained argument. Schopenhauer made a version of the point two centuries earlier, when he wrote that the will gets into the sentence before the intellect has finished arranging it.

prose_in: If a system of that kind ever did emerge, the work it could do wouldn't stop at mediating between existing communities. It could also, in principle, point at places where the shared human embedding itself is wrong: places where the language people reason in tends to push them toward a particular conclusion, the conclusion fails on closer examination, and nobody reasoning inside that language is positioned to notice the failure, since the language itself is part of what's producing it. Whether such places exist at all, and whether any future system would be in a position to expose them if they do, are open questions. The conjecture is still worth naming, since if it turns out to be even partly correct, the long-run picture of what mediation work is doing has to be drawn differently.

prose_in: Neither side was missing something obvious about the scene. Both saw zebras on a finite stretch of grassland. The disagreement was about what was supposed to follow from that. The biologist had spent years watching the regional zebra population decline, and to him the herd in front of him was a species worth keeping alive. The pastoralists had spent the same dry seasons watching those animals eat grass their cattle and goats couldn't afford to lose, and to them the same herd represented another bad year. Both groups were using "grassland" in the way English-speakers use the word; what differed was what each side had built on top of it — the chain of consequence about what should happen given a finite grassland ran in opposite directions for the two groups.

prose_in: The reason programming languages got globally adopted is that people use them to make machines (which demand logical precision) do specific things, and the cross-cultural readability turned out to be a side effect of that more practical motivation. The downside is that they only handle what is already shaped like a procedure: an algorithm, a data pipeline, a piece of arithmetic. Disagreements about values are much harder to put into code. The only way to put a value-disagreement in is to reduce it to an algorithmic decision, and the reduction tends to drop the part of the disagreement that was actually in dispute. There is also the access problem: most of the people whose disagreements matter most for community-to-community translation will never write a line of code, and have no particular reason to, just like with any other formal logic language.

There is also the access problem: most of those whose disagreements matter for community-to-community translation will never write a line of code, and have no reason to.

prose_in: That much is technical. What follows from it, for the present argument, depends on a conditional which I would rather state plainly than try to smuggle past the reader. If training has only memorised surface co-occurrences between words, the model is a parrot, and most of the rest of this essay collapses. If, instead, training has actually picked up durable patterns in how humans express their reasoning, what's been built is an instrument of a kind that didn't exist before. The systems we currently have do not let us pick cleanly between those two readings. Outputs are uneven: some look clearly like memorisation, others clearly don't, and a large stretch in the middle has no clean test. The same prompt run twice can produce a useful argument map on Monday and, on Wednesday, a smoother version that has quietly dropped a premise the rest of the structure was meant to rest on. The current models hallucinate, flatter their users, and offer confident wrong answers rather than admitting they don't know. None of these failures, taken on its own, settles the larger question.
